description: Already intellectually sold on the program but paralysed by the ₹22.65L price tag relative to their current financial reality. They want to join — they just need a credible financial pathway to justify the leap.
specific_objection_pattern: ₹22.65L is genuinely unaffordable without a substantial scholarship (40–60%+), EMI flexibility, or need-based support; they will not commit until the financial equation feels survivable
conversion_trigger: A concrete scholarship pathway or escalation to a senior admissions figure who can personalise the fee conversation — combined with reframing the ₹500 application as a low-risk first step that unlocks the scholarship negotiation, not a financial commitment
emotional_arc: Already-interested → Stalled on fee → Soft-committed after scholarship pathway opened
counsellor_playbook: On call 1, acknowledge the fee concern immediately and validate it rather than deflecting — say 'that's a fair concern and here's exactly how the scholarship process works.' Reframe the application fee as the entry ticket to the scholarship conversation, not a down payment on ₹22.65L. If the candidate signals genuine financial hardship (self-funded, all income in upskilling, family dependency), escalate to Head of Admissions for a personalised scholarship call within 24 hours — do not leave this floating. Never promise a specific scholarship percentage, but do make clear that need-based cases are reviewed seriously and that applying is the only way to open that door.
example_journey_summary: Journey 2: IIT student self-funded and half-committed on Call 1, stalled repeatedly on fee until escalation to Head of Admissions Sonia Jain unlocked the scholarship conversation and moved him toward conversion.
do_not_do: Do not paper over the fee with generic 'it's an investment' ROI talk before they feel heard — this persona has already done the math and it doesn't work yet. Never leave the scholarship question vague or deferred beyond the first two calls; ambiguity here reads as evasion and kills trust.

description: Arrives already researched, already motivated, and functionally pre-converted — often via referral, Instagram, campus visit, or their own due diligence. The counsellor's primary job is operational unblocking, not persuasion.
specific_objection_pattern: Not a genuine objection to the program — rather, logistical friction: missed deadlines, busy schedules, video essay confusion, upload errors, or work overload causing application steps to stall
conversion_trigger: A direct, time-bound nudge from the counsellor that removes the specific operational blocker — resending a link, scheduling a Google Meet to fix a CV upload glitch, or giving a same-day deadline with a specific action step
emotional_arc: Already-interested → Operationally blocked → Converted once friction removed
counsellor_playbook: Diagnose within the first 60 seconds whether this person is already sold — if they've researched the program, mention peer referrals, or show no real objection to value, stop pitching and switch to a process-completion mode. Identify the single blocking step (video essay, aptitude test, CV upload) and solve it on the call — send the link, walk them through the dashboard, or book a follow-up Meet for the same day. Use deadline urgency as the closing tool: give a specific date and time, not a vague 'whenever you get a chance.' Log every promised action and follow through — this persona converts fast but drops off equally fast if they hit a dead end with no support.
example_journey_summary: Journey 4: Already-converted professional stalled across six calls solely due to work overload blocking video essay submission — persistent follow-up nudges were the only intervention needed.
do_not_do: Do not re-pitch program value or curriculum to someone who is already sold — it wastes their time and signals that you haven't listened. Never let an operational blocker (missed deadline, broken link, upload confusion) sit unresolved across multiple calls; each unresolved blocker increases dropout risk significantly.

description: Genuinely interested but wrestling with whether they personally belong in the program — whether their background (non-tech, B.Com, MBA, fresher, academia) disqualifies them or whether the program's outcomes match their specific career direction.
specific_objection_pattern: Background mismatch anxiety (non-BCA/B.Tech profile, MBA wondering if it's too technical, fresher unsure of eligibility) or role-direction confusion (wants AI + management, not pure technical; questions whether a certification beats a degree)
conversion_trigger: Counsellor directly and specifically validates their profile as not just eligible but strategically well-suited — ideally connecting their exact background to a named outcome (e.g., 'AI PM role' for an MBA, 'business-building track' for an agency founder) rather than giving generic eligibility reassurance
emotional_arc: Skeptical-to-curious → Reassured on fit → Committed once personal relevance clicked
counsellor_playbook: Lead with discovery: ask about their background and career goal before pitching anything, so you can mirror their language back at them when validating fit. Make the fit argument specific and personal — 'your B.Com plus MBA background is exactly the profile that moves into AI PM or AI strategy roles, which is the program's fastest-growing placement track' — not a generic 'we accept all backgrounds.' If they raise the degree-vs-certification concern, address it directly with placement outcomes data rather than dodging it. Use a Socratic question — 'If you applied for an AI role today, what gap would you hit?' — to let them self-identify the problem the program solves, which creates intrinsic motivation rather than counsellor-imposed urgency.
example_journey_summary: Journey 32: Alumni-referred mid-career professional with an MBA questioned whether another program added value until the counsellor directly connected his hybrid background to AI PM roles, making fit feel personally inevitable.
do_not_do: Do not give blanket eligibility reassurance ('yes, all backgrounds are welcome') without connecting it to a specific outcome for their profile — vague validation does not resolve fit anxiety. Never skip discovery and jump to pitch; if you do not know their background and goal, your fit argument will feel scripted and unconvincing.

description: High-intent but holding back due to institutional skepticism — the program is the first batch, there is no placement track record, the fee is steep for an unproven outcome, and they need a credible signal before they will move. Often mid-career or experienced professionals with something to lose.
specific_objection_pattern: First-batch risk with no placement proof, comparing Masters Union against ISB/IIT brand equity, wanting campus visit before any commitment, or process failures (unanswered calls, wrong counsellor, spam numbers) eroding institutional credibility
conversion_trigger: A tangible credibility signal delivered personally — campus visit invitation, a named senior contact (Head of Admissions), placement process walkthrough with specifics, or a direct peer comparison that reframes Masters Union's first-mover positioning as an advantage rather than a liability
emotional_arc: Already-interested but guarded → Credibility signal received → Conditionally committed
counsellor_playbook: Acknowledge the first-batch concern proactively rather than waiting for them to raise it — 'I know you're probably wondering about placement track record since this is the inaugural cohort; let me tell you exactly how we're structuring that.' Offer a campus visit as an early-funnel move, but reframe it as parallel to the application rather than a prerequisite — 'you can book the visit and apply simultaneously, neither blocks the other.' If process failures have occurred (missed calls, wrong counsellor, spam numbers), open with a direct apology and give them a named point of contact going forward — trust repair is the first order of business. Use ISB/IIT comparisons as an opening, not a threat: ask what specifically they valued about those programs, then show how Masters Union's model addresses those same needs.
example_journey_summary: Journey 42: Experienced professional comparing Masters Union to ISB and IIT self-converted in one call after the counsellor offered a campus visit hook and closed the application ask cleanly without overcomplicating the pitch.
do_not_do: Do not dismiss or minimise the first-batch concern with defensive enthusiasm — saying 'we have amazing faculty and industry partners' without addressing placement proof directly makes skepticism worse. Never let process failures (unanswered calls, wrong counsellor assignment, spam number appearances) go unacknowledged; they are the fastest way to confirm the institutional distrust this persona already fears.

description: Appears in the CRM as a conversion but left no meaningful sales signal in the recorded call journey — converted via unrecorded in-person interactions, off-channel conversations, or pure self-motivation with no counsellor contribution. These journeys reveal process gaps, not sales skill.
specific_objection_pattern: No objection pattern detectable — calls are voicemails, wrong-time contacts, zero-content interactions, or operational logistics only; the actual conversion decision happened entirely outside the recorded call journey
conversion_trigger: Cannot be determined from available data — conversion was driven by unrecorded campus visits, peer referrals, off-channel WhatsApp conversations, or the candidate's own prior research with no counsellor involvement
emotional_arc: Unknown — no emotional arc detectable in recorded calls
counsellor_playbook: When you recognise this pattern early (voicemail on call 1, wrong-time contact, zero engagement), do not burn call attempts — switch channels immediately to WhatsApp with a short message that invites a reply at their convenience. If a campus visit is happening, ensure it is logged and debriefed — the in-person interaction is clearly doing the heavy lifting that calls cannot. Use the operational touchpoints you do have (video essay reminders, deadline nudges) to stay present without being intrusive. After conversion, conduct a brief 'what decided it for you' check-in to capture the actual conversion driver for future training.
example_journey_summary: Journey 7: High-intent post-MUSAT candidate made a campus visit and converted entirely off-record — nine calls logged with zero substantive sales content, conversion happened entirely in person.
do_not_do: Do not treat these journeys as normal sales successes and attribute them to counsellor skill — the pattern masks a data capture problem that inflates conversion metrics without generating replicable learnings. Never continue burning call attempts on a ghost journey without switching channels; repeated unanswered calls train candidates to ignore your number.
